sections.paragraph:
or מַת not used in sing. a man (Eth. ምት፡ a man, specially a husband). In the Hebrew language itself, traces of a singular number are found in the pr.n. מְתוּשָׁאֵל, מְתוּשֶׁלַת (מְתוּ is a construct form, like אָב Chald. constr. אֲבוּ; שֵׁם, שְׁמוּ, whence שְׁמוּאֵל; פָּנִים in sing. const. פְּנוּ, whence פְּנוּאֵל), and also in the Punic, as Metuastartus (מְתוּ עַשְׁתֹּרֶת) i.e. man or worshipper of Astarte, Muthumballes, i.e. מְתוּבַעַל man of Baal.

Pl. מְתִים, defect. מְתִם m. men, (not of the common gender); Deu 2:34, מְתִים וְהַנָּשִׁים וְהַטָּף “men and women and children;” Deu 3:6 Job 11:3 Isa 3:25, מְתֵי מִסְפָּר a few men, Gen 34:30 מְתֵי שָׁוְא “men of falsehood,” Psa 26:4 מְתֵי אָהֳלִי “the men of my tabernacle,” Job 31:31.- Isa 41:14, the words מְתֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל are well rendered by Luther, du armer Haufe Israel, LXX. ὀλιγοστὸς Ἰσραήλ; but this notion of misery and fewness is not in the word מְתֵי, but is taken from the preceding תֹּולַעַת.
